title: The Payor Mix Question Every Allied Health Practice Faces at EOFY
linkedin:
Most allied health practices know June is their biggest billing month.

What fewer have looked at is whether the surge actually landed at the margins the practice needed.

The PHI extras reset drives the June booking rush. Patients book before their annual entitlement disappears on 1 July. The appointment book fills. Revenue at month end looks strong.

But two things quietly complicate that picture.

Not all June billing settles in June. Telehealth claims and manual PHI submissions can take five to fifteen business days to process. For practices with high telehealth volume, a portion of June's clinical work lands in July's account.

And billable ratio doesn't improve automatically because the schedule looks full. Administration, travel, and referrer coordination sit within paid hours but outside billed ones.

The more revealing EOFY question is what the payor mix looks like across the full twelve months.

Revenue per clinician hour differs across billing streams. Medicare rebates are fixed by the MBS schedule regardless of complexity. Privately funded sessions at the practice's own rate generate a different figure. When the mix shifts toward Medicare-funded referrals over time, the effective rate stays flat while costs move.

The twelve months of data available right now show that gap clearly. Understanding which billing streams drove the margin, not just the revenue, is what shapes the scheduling and referral decisions that matter for FY27.

Full piece on the ProfitPulse blog. Link in the first comment.

facebook:
For physio, psychology, OT, and podiatry practice owners, June is typically the biggest billing month of the year as patients rush to use PHI extras before July 1.

But there's a question worth sitting with before the new year starts: did June's revenue come from the billing streams that actually deliver the margin the practice needs?

Revenue per clinician hour differs significantly across Medicare items, private health insurance, NDIS, and direct private billing. When the mix shifts toward lower-rate streams over time, the headline revenue figure keeps growing while margin per hour quietly compresses.

If you haven't mapped your payor mix across the full year yet, EOFY is the natural moment. Three days to go.

#AustralianBusiness #SMEFinance
google:
For physiotherapy, psychology, occupational therapy, podiatry, and speech pathology practice owners on the east coast, the end of the financial year is the most revealing moment in the practice calendar.

The June booking surge driven by private health insurance extras limits brings the highest billing volume of the year. But the total revenue figure does not automatically show which billing streams generated the margin, and for many practices, the payor mix has shifted over recent years without a deliberate decision to move it.

Medicare item rebates are set by the MBS schedule. When more of the practice's billing comes from Medicare-funded referrals over time, the effective hourly rate stays fixed while costs move with award increases, rent, and superannuation. Revenue grows. Margin per clinical hour does not keep pace.

With the financial year closing on 30 June, the twelve months of billing data now available are the clearest picture a practice owner will have all year. Running a breakdown of revenue by payor type across the full year, and comparing contribution per clinician hour across each stream, shows which referral channels are generating the margin and which have quietly drifted.

ProfitPulse works with allied health and clinical practice owners across Queensland, New South Wales, and Victoria on this kind of financial clarity. Read the full piece on the ProfitPulse blog.
